headline: Rural North Carolina Opens Every Door to Addiction Treatment
meta_description: A new network in North Carolina is making addiction medicine part of primary care, reaching 88 counties and serving thousands with medication-based treatment.
summary_short: North Carolina's NC STAR Network is transforming addiction care by embedding it into primary care clinics across rural communities, removing stigma and expanding access.
body:
A shift is quietly reshaping how rural North Carolina treats addiction. The NC STAR Network, a partnership among medical schools and community health centers, has made medication for opioid use disorder available through ordinary primary care offices—the places where patients already go for diabetes checks and blood pressure management. The network now reaches 88 of North Carolina's 100 counties, with spoke sites serving well over 4,000 patients in the past year. By treating addiction as part of whole-person care rather than as a separate, specialized service, the initiative is making treatment less shameful and more accessible to people who need it.

The approach emerged from a deliberate choice. When the federal government lifted restrictions on buprenorphine administration in 2023, North Carolina's medical leaders—including Dr. Claire West at UNC and Dr. Gabriela Castro in rural Chatham County—recognized an opportunity. They designed a hub-and-spoke model inspired by Vermont's success, with academic medical centers and the Mountain Area Health Education Center training and supporting primary care clinics throughout the state. The relationship works both ways: community practitioners tell hub staff what actually works on the ground, shaping how the program evolves.

The benefits are measurable and human. Patients who receive addiction treatment in primary care settings have retention rates equal to specialty clinics but report greater satisfaction. A primary care office feels less stigmatizing. As one provider put it, a patient receiving treatment for opioid use disorder can simultaneously begin HIV prevention and hepatitis screening in the same visit—and a patient struggling with homelessness can be connected to housing resources. When addiction treatment lives in the same waiting room as everything else, it stops feeling shameful. It becomes, simply, part of care.
quick_facts: NC STAR Network reaches 88 of North Carolina's 100 counties
Spoke sites served over 4,000 patients in July 2024–June 2025
Hub-and-spoke model inspired by Vermont's approach
Mountain Area Health Education Center serves rural Western North Carolina
